text: The surgeon gains access to the peritoneal cavity, with multiple robotic trocars visibly inserted into the abdominal wall.

The surgeon observes radiation-related changes along the right pelvic side wall, with visible adhesions involving the upper rectum, sigmoid colon, and terminal ileum attached to the pelvic structures.

The surgeon uses a hook cautery to carefully lyse adhesions and attachments in the area of the right pelvic side wall, where radiation-related changes are visible in the upper rectum, sigmoid colon, and terminal ileum.
The surgeon exercises caution to avoid injuring the small intestine during the robotic low anterior resection procedure.
The surgeon attempts to start a medial to lateral dissection but is delayed due to pelvic adhesions, which require careful lysing before proceeding.
The surgeon frees the small bowel from the pelvis, ensuring it is completely mobilized before proceeding with the next steps of the low anterior resection.
The surgeon utilizes a third robotic arm to assist in the rectal dissection, enhancing precision and control during the procedure.

The small bowel is visibly moved out of the pelvis, and the surgeon incises the peritoneum along the right lateral edge of the sigmoid and rectum. A plane is developed between the mesorectum and retroperitoneal structures.
The surgeon uses a hook cautery to carefully lyse dense adhesions, which are limiting progress in the dissection of the rectal area.
A lighted ureteral stent is visible, intended to facilitate dissection. The stent is malpositioned, located only in the very distal ureter.
The surgeon continues dissection to open the holy plane, while progress is slow. Attention shifts to the lateral attachments of the sigmoid colon, where careful separation is performed.
The surgeon uses a hook cautery to precisely dissect tissue, allowing gas to enter and expand the space behind the peritoneum, aiding in the separation of tissue layers.
The surgeon uses hook cautery to perform a layer-by-layer dissection, carefully avoiding damage to surrounding structures and achieving precise separation of tissue layers.
The surgeon maneuvers between the medial and lateral views to navigate the sticky and fibrotic post-radiation tissues, visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon works along the left side of the proximal rectum, dissecting the remaining attachments between the mesosigmoid and retroperitoneal structures.

The surgeon shifts focus back to the medial view, navigating the dense fibrotic tissue between the retroperitoneum and the mesentery of the sigmoid colon and rectum, using a to-and-fro motion to manage the challenging dissection.
The surgeon skeletonizes the pedicle and uses a stapler to transect it, noting the thickness of the pedicle. The stapler provides effective hemostasis, similar to a vessel sealing device.

The surgeon uses a hook cautery to carefully lyse an adhesion tethering the right lateral aspect of the colon, proceeding in a layer-by-layer fashion before entering the pelvis. The tumor is located significantly lower in the pelvic region.

The surgeon is approximately 2.5 centimeters from the anal verge, working through post-radiation changes. Traction and counter-traction are used to reveal normal anatomic planes, with the assistant providing additional counter-traction to aid in the dissection.

The surgeon uses a third robotic arm to wrap around and lift the colon, providing anterior traction on the rectum and elevating it out of the pelvis. This maneuver enhances exposure in the confined pelvic space, allowing additional instruments to open the natural anatomic planes posteriorly.

The surgeon demonstrates a technique to rapidly reach low areas in the pelvis, initially dissecting directly posteriorly, then circumferentially around the lateral stalks, and finally moving anteriorly.
The surgeon places a folded ratex into the pelvis to absorb and remove remaining areas of fluid, facilitating clearer visualization of the surgical field.

The surgeon uses a hook to achieve hemostasis while following the normal anatomic planes. An air seal device is employed to clear smoke in the confined surgical space, with an 8mm port positioned in the right upper quadrant serving as the air seal device.
The surgeon applies lateral tension toward the right lateral stalk, developing the anatomical plane in this region.
The surgeon carefully establishes traction and counter traction to follow the normal anatomic planes, allowing for the opening of the left lateral stalk and attachments to the rectum.
The surgeon focuses on the anterior dissection of the mesorectum, using a robotic arm to provide counter-traction by pulling the prostate anteriorly, facilitating the dissection process.
The surgeon follows the normal anatomical planes within the narrow male pelvis, utilizing robotic trocars to facilitate dissection without arm collisions using the DaVinci XI robot.
The surgeon performs circumferential dissection in a to-and-fro manner, progressively reaching down to the pelvic floor, with visible manipulation of surrounding tissues.
The surgeon exposes the seminal vesicle and vas deferens during anterior dissection. Dissection proceeds along the right side, revealing muscle fibers of the levator ani.
The surgeon reaches the pelvic floor and performs additional posterior dissection to further expose the area, utilizing robotic assistance for precise maneuvering.

The surgeon performs final adjustments in the intra-abdominal portion of the low anterior resection before transitioning to the perineal portion, which will be conducted in an open fashion with the patient in the lithotomy position.

The surgeon identifies the ureter within the retroperitoneum, visible with blinking illumination. The planned site for colonic transection is marked using Firefly imaging, and a stapling device is used for the transection. A colostomy is brought out through a trocar site in the left lower quadrant, with lateral attachments being released to gain additional length for the colostomy.
